content.text: What Zone-Redundant Means

A zone-redundant service is one where the Azure platform automatically distributes the resource's instances, replicas, or shards across all Availability Zones in the region. You provision once; the platform handles zone placement. If one zone goes offline, the remaining zones continue serving traffic without any operator action.

This contrasts with zonal (zone-pinned) resources, where you pick a specific zone and the resource lives only there. Zone-redundant services absorb zone failures silently at the platform level, while zonal designs push that responsibility to the application architect.
No zone selection required:
you do not pass a --zone flag. The platform places copies in all zones automatically.
Synchronous or near-synchronous replication:
data is written across zones before the write is acknowledged (for storage and databases), keeping RPO near zero.
Active-active, not active-passive:
all zone copies serve traffic concurrently in normal operation, giving load distribution in addition to redundancy.
99.99% SLA (typical):
zone-redundant tiers generally carry a four-nine SLA compared to a three-nine SLA for non-redundant equivalents.

attrs.alt: Zone-Redundant Services distributing automatically across three Availability Zones

content.text: Zone-Redundant Services Reference
The following Azure services support zone-redundant deployment. In most cases zone-redundancy is a configuration option (a SKU tier, a flag, or a feature toggle) rather than the default.
Service
Zone-Redundant Option
Key Note
Azure Blob / ADLS Gen2 / Files
ZRS and GZRS replication tiers
ZRS = synchronous 3-zone; GZRS also replicates to paired region
Azure SQL Database
zone_redundant=true on Business Critical / General Purpose
Synchronous quorum write across zones
Azure SQL Managed Instance
zone_redundant=true (Business Critical)
Requires Business Critical tier; General Purpose not zone-redundant
Azure Cosmos DB
Availability Zones toggle per region
Multi-write regions each independently zone-redundant
Azure Cache for Redis
Availability Zones config on Premium/Enterprise
At least 2 zones recommended; data replicated across zone replicas
Event Hubs Premium/Dedicated
Zone redundancy enabled by default on Premium
Standard tier does not support AZs
Service Bus Premium
Zone redundancy on Premium tier
Standard tier does not support AZs
Azure App Gateway v2
Deployed across zones by default in supported regions
v1 is single-zone; always use v2 for zone resilience
Standard Load Balancer
Zone-redundant frontend IP (omit --zone flag)
Basic LB has no AZ support; use Standard SKU
Azure Key Vault
Automatic in AZ-enabled regions
No configuration needed; built into the service
AKS control plane
Always zone-redundant in supported regions
Node pools must be spread with --zones per pool
Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible
zone_redundant_ha=true
Standby in a different zone; synchronous replication
Azure Container Apps
Zone redundancy toggle in environment
Requires at least 3 replicas to distribute across zones
Enabling Zone Redundancy: Core Services
Azure Storage: ZRS and GZRS
Zone-Redundant Storage (ZRS) writes each object synchronously to three storage clusters, one per zone. A zone failure causes no data loss and no failover — the remaining clusters keep serving.

# Create a storage account with ZRS replication
az storage account create \
  --name mystorageacct \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_ZRS \
  --kind StorageV2

# Upgrade an existing LRS account to ZRS (live migration, no downtime, may take hours)
az storage account update \
  --name mystorageacct \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --sku Standard_ZRS

# Check current replication type
az storage account show \
  --name mystorageacct \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query sku
ZRS to GZRS is also a live migration. GZRS adds asynchronous replication to the paired region on top of the synchronous cross-zone writes, giving both zone and regional protection in a single SKU.
Azure SQL Database: Zone-Redundant HA

Azure SQL Database's zone-redundant option places the primary replica and at least two HA secondaries in different zones. Writes use synchronous quorum: the transaction is not committed until a majority of replicas acknowledge it. Failover to a secondary in another zone takes seconds and is transparent to the application connection string.

# Create a zone-redundant SQL Database (Business Critical or General Purpose)
az sql db create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name mydb \
  --edition BusinessCritical \
  --family Gen5 \
  --capacity 8 \
  --zone-redundant true

# Enable zone redundancy on an existing database
az sql db update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name mydb \
  --zone-redundant true

# Confirm zone redundancy is active
az sql db show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name mydb \
  --query zoneRedundant
Azure Cosmos DB: Per-Region Zone Redundancy

Cosmos DB lets you enable AZ redundancy independently for each region. Writes are synchronously committed to a quorum of replicas within the zone-redundant cluster before acknowledged. Zone failures in any configured region do not interrupt global write availability.

# Enable zone redundancy when adding regions to a Cosmos DB account
az cosmosdb update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mycosmosaccount \
  --locations regionName=eastus failoverPriority=0 isZoneRedundant=true \
             regionName=westus failoverPriority=1 isZoneRedundant=true

# Verify zone redundancy per location
az cosmosdb show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mycosmosaccount \
  --query "locations[*].{region:locationName, zr:isZoneRedundant}" \
  --output table
Standard Load Balancer: Zone-Redundant Frontend IP

A Standard Load Balancer frontend IP can be zone-redundant, meaning the IP is served from all three zones simultaneously. Traffic keeps flowing through the remaining zones if one fails, with no IP failover.

# Create a zone-redundant Public IP (Standard SKU with no --zone = zone-redundant)
az network public-ip create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myPublicIP \
  --sku Standard \
  --allocation-method Static

# Create Standard LB with zone-redundant frontend
az network lb create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myLB \
  --sku Standard \
  --public-ip-address myPublicIP \
  --frontend-ip-name myFrontend \
  --backend-pool-name myBackendPool

# Confirm frontend zone configuration (null = zone-redundant)
az network lb frontend-ip show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --lb-name myLB \
  --name myFrontend \
  --query zones
Service Bus and Event Hubs: Messaging Zone Redundancy
For messaging services, zone redundancy is tied to the pricing tier. Service Bus Premium and Event Hubs Premium/Dedicated automatically enable zone redundancy; Standard does not support it at all.

# Create a zone-redundant Service Bus namespace (Premium tier required)
az servicebus namespace create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mySBNamespace \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Premium \
  --zone-redundant true

# Create a zone-redundant Event Hubs namespace (Premium tier)
az eventhubs namespace create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myEHNamespace \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Premium \
  --zone-redundant true

# Verify zone-redundant flag
az servicebus namespace show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mySBNamespace \
  --query zoneRedundant
Zone-Redundant vs Zonal: When to Use Each

Zone-redundant and zonal services are not mutually exclusive — typical architectures combine both. A zone-redundant load balancer distributes incoming traffic to zonal VMs spread manually across zones. The key is understanding which tier of your stack needs platform-managed redundancy and which needs manual zone spreading.
Dimension
Zone-Redundant
Zonal (Zone-Pinned)
Zone placement
Platform distributes automatically
You specify --zone 1/2/3 explicitly
Zone failure response
Automatic: remaining zones serve traffic
Manual: architect must spread resources and handle failover
Typical use
Managed services: storage, databases, LBs, messaging
Compute: VMs, VMSS, NVAs needing explicit zone awareness
Latency overhead
Cross-zone writes absorbed by platform transparently
Zero within a zone; cross-zone traffic is visible and charged
Cost
Premium SKU often required
Cross-zone data transfer $0.01/GB when traffic crosses zones
RPO / RTO
Near-zero RPO; near-zero RTO (no failover needed)
Depends on architecture; manual failover could be minutes
Config effort
Single flag or SKU selection
Zone mapping required for every resource in the stack
IaC: Declaring Zone Redundancy
Bicep
Terraform

# ZRS Storage Account
resource "azurerm_storage_account" "example" {
  name                     = "mystorageacct"
  resource_group_name      = azurerm_resource_group.example.name
  location                 = azurerm_resource_group.example.location
  account_tier             = "Standard"
  account_replication_type = "ZRS"   # Zone-Redundant Storage
  min_tls_version          = "TLS1_2"
}

# Zone-redundant SQL Database
resource "azurerm_mssql_database" "example" {
  name           = "mydb"
  server_id      = azurerm_mssql_server.example.id
  sku_name       = "BC_Gen5_8"   # Business Critical required for zone redundancy
  zone_redundant = true
}

# Zone-redundant Service Bus (Premium)
resource "azurerm_servicebus_namespace" "example" {
  name                = "mySBNamespace"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.example.name
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.example.location
  sku                 = "Premium"
  zone_redundant      = true
}

# Standard LB public IP (zone-redundant by omitting zones)
resource "azurerm_public_ip" "lb_ip" {
  name                = "myLBPublicIP"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.example.name
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.example.location
  allocation_method   = "Static"
  sku                 = "Standard"
  # zones not set -> zone-redundant
}
Verifying Zone-Redundant Configuration
Before relying on zone redundancy in production, confirm that each resource has it enabled. The following script checks the most common zone-redundant services in a resource group:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Usage: ./check-zr.sh <resource-group>
RG="${1:-myRG}"

echo "=== Storage accounts (replication type) ==="
az storage account list -g "$RG" \
  --query "[*].{name:name, sku:sku.name}" \
  --output table

echo ""
echo "=== SQL Databases (zoneRedundant flag) ==="
az sql server list -g "$RG" --query "[*].name" -o tsv | while read SERVER; do
  echo "Server: $SERVER"
  az sql db list -g "$RG" --server "$SERVER" \
    --query "[*].{db:name, zr:zoneRedundant}" --output table
done

echo ""
echo "=== Cosmos DB accounts (AZ per location) ==="
az cosmosdb list -g "$RG" \
  --query "[*].{account:name, locs:locations[*].{region:locationName,zr:isZoneRedundant}}" \
  --output json

echo ""
echo "=== Service Bus namespaces (zoneRedundant) ==="
az servicebus namespace list -g "$RG" \
  --query "[*].{name:name, sku:sku.name, zr:zoneRedundant}" \
  --output table
Run this audit after every major deployment to catch non-zone-redundant resources before they become a reliability gap in production.
Common Pitfalls
Basic SKU does not support AZs:
Azure Basic Load Balancer, Basic Public IP, and non-Standard storage SKUs have no zone support. Always use Standard SKU.
Premium tier required for most databases:
Azure SQL zone redundancy requires Business Critical or General Purpose tier. Basic and Standard tiers have no AZ support.
ZRS is regional only:
ZRS protects against zone failures but not regional outages. Pair ZRS with geo-replication (GZRS) for regional DR.
Service Bus Standard has no zone support:
Only Premium tier supports AZs. Messaging workloads requiring zone resilience must use Premium.
Event Hubs Standard has no zone support:
Premium and Dedicated tiers include zone redundancy; Standard does not.
Redis requires Premium tier for AZs:
Basic and Standard Azure Cache for Redis tiers run on single nodes and do not support zone redundancy.
Container Apps require 3+ replicas:
Enabling zone redundancy on a Container Apps environment does nothing for an app with fewer than 3 replicas.
AKS zone config is per node pool:
The --zones flag must be set on each node pool individually; the zone-redundant control plane does not spread node pools automatically.
Monitoring Zone Health
Azure Monitor and Service Health provide visibility into zone-level events. Proactive monitoring lets you detect zone degradation before it surfaces as user-visible errors.

# Create a Service Health alert for AZ-related outages in East US
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name "AZ-health-alert" \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)" \
  --condition "category=ServiceHealth" \
  --action-groups /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/myAG

# Check resource health for a zone-redundant storage account
az rest \
  --method get \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mystorageacct/providers/Microsoft.ResourceHealth/availabilityStatuses/current?api-version=2020-01-01" \
  --query "properties.{status:availabilityState, summary:summary}"

# List availability status for all resources in a resource group
az rest \
  --method get \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.ResourceHealth/availabilityStatuses?api-version=2020-01-01" \
  --query "value[?properties.availabilityState!='Available'].{resource:id, status:properties.availabilityState}" \
  --output table
Zone-Redundant Architecture Patterns

Zone-redundant managed services work best when paired with zone-spread compute. The goal is to eliminate every single point of failure at the zone level across the full request path: DNS, load balancing, compute, caching, messaging, and persistence.
Three-Tier Zone-Redundant Stack
A typical web application stack uses zone-redundant services at every managed layer and zone-spread VMs or containers at the compute layer:

# 1. Zone-redundant Application Gateway v2 (handles zone spread automatically)
az network application-gateway create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAppGW \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_v2 \
  --min-capacity 2 \
  --max-capacity 10 \
  --public-ip-address myPublicIP \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet appgwSubnet

# 2. Zone-spread VMSS (compute layer — each zone gets its own instance group)
az vmss create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVMSS \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --vm-sku Standard_DS2_v2 \
  --instance-count 3 \
  --zones 1 2 3 \
  --lb "" \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet appSubnet

# 3. Zone-redundant Azure SQL Database as the data tier
az sql db create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name mydb \
  --edition GeneralPurpose \
  --family Gen5 \
  --capacity 4 \
  --zone-redundant true
Zone-Redundant AKS with Zone-Redundant Backing Services
For containerised workloads, AKS node pools with --zones spread pods across zones while the AKS control plane is always zone-redundant. Pair with ZRS persistent volumes and zone-redundant databases:

# Create AKS cluster with zone-spread system node pool
az aks create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAKSCluster \
  --node-count 3 \
  --zones 1 2 3 \
  --enable-managed-identity \
  --network-plugin azure

# Add a zone-spread user node pool
az aks nodepool add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --cluster-name myAKSCluster \
  --name userpool \
  --node-count 3 \
  --zones 1 2 3

# Create a ZRS-backed StorageClass for PVCs (persists across zone failures)
# storageclass.yaml:
# apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
# kind: StorageClass
# metadata:
#   name: azuredisk-zrs
# provisioner: disk.csi.azure.com
# parameters:
#   skuname: Premium_ZRS
# volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer
SLA Composition: Adding Zone-Redundant Layers
Azure SLAs for zone-redundant services are multiplicative across the stack. A zone-redundant application must combine the SLAs of every component in the path to get the composite SLA.
Component
Non-Zone-Redundant SLA
Zone-Redundant SLA
Azure Blob Storage
Azure SQL Database (General Purpose)
99.995% (zone-redundant)
Standard Load Balancer
99.99% (zone-redundant frontend)
App Gateway v2
99.95% (automatically zone-redundant)
Service Bus Premium
99.95% (zone-redundant)
Azure Cache for Redis Premium
99.99% (with zone redundancy)
AKS (control plane)
99.95% (control plane always zone-redundant)

To calculate the composite SLA for a chain of independent components, multiply their individual SLAs. A three-component chain of 99.99% x 99.99% x 99.995% yields approximately 99.975% — still well within four-nine territory. Adding non-zone-redundant components (99.9%) to the chain quickly degrades the composite SLA.

Every non-zone-redundant component in the critical path becomes the reliability ceiling for the entire application. Zone-redundant managed services raise that ceiling; non-redundant links pull it back down.
Testing Zone-Redundant Behaviour

Zone redundancy is not guaranteed just because a flag is set. Validate that traffic continues flowing during a simulated zone failure using Azure Chaos Studio or by temporarily restricting traffic to a single zone:

# Azure Chaos Studio: fault injection targeting a zone
# 1. Enable Chaos Studio on the target resource
az rest \
  --method put \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mystorageacct/providers/Microsoft.Chaos/targets/Microsoft-StorageAccount?api-version=2023-11-01" \
  --body '{"location":"eastus","properties":{}}'

# 2. Add the zone fault capability
az rest \
  --method put \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mystorageacct/providers/Microsoft.Chaos/targets/Microsoft-StorageAccount/capabilities/ZoneTransient-1.0?api-version=2023-11-01" \
  --body '{}'

# For manual validation without Chaos Studio:
# - Monitor error rates from Azure Monitor during planned maintenance windows
# - Deploy a canary probe that writes to storage every 30 seconds and alerts on failure
# - Use Azure Load Testing to generate load during zone-level simulations
Run load tests during simulated zone outages to confirm throughput does not drop below acceptable thresholds.
Verify that application connection strings and SDK retry policies handle brief zone-transition latency (typically sub-second) without surfacing errors to users.
Confirm that monitoring dashboards show no persistent error rate during zone tests — transient spikes under 1 second are acceptable; sustained errors indicate a misconfiguration.
Document the observed RTO from zone-failure detection to full traffic resumption; for true zone-redundant services this should be measurable in milliseconds.
Summary

Zone-redundant services let Azure manage cross-zone distribution automatically. You opt in with a SKU or flag, and the platform ensures data is replicated synchronously across all zones, load balancers serve from all zones, and databases maintain quorum writes without requiring application-level failover logic.
Use ZRS or GZRS for storage to get synchronous three-zone writes with near-zero RPO on zone failure.
Enable zone_redundant=true on SQL Database (Business Critical / General Purpose) and Cosmos DB for database-tier zone resilience.
Standard Load Balancer and App Gateway v2 provide zone-redundant traffic ingress; Basic SKU does not support AZs.
Service Bus Premium and Event Hubs Premium/Dedicated include zone redundancy; Standard tiers do not.
Combine zone-redundant managed services with zonal (zone-spread) compute for a full-stack zone-resilient architecture.
Run periodic audits with az CLI to confirm zone-redundant flags are actually set on all critical resources.
